context:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Syllabus:
11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning

Course Meeting Times:
- Seminar: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session

Course Description:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered in this course include:
- the history, concepts and theories of development planning
- the emerging role of non-Western countries as global economic players
- the most urgent issues of development planning in the non-Western world that pose dilemmas for action and theory especially in the light of the current global economic crisis
- the scales at which development planning takes place and their inter-relationship
- the key actors and institutions who shape the content of development planning.

The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Several themes run through the course. One such theme is the intimate link between planning, power and legitimacy, chiefly embodied in the state. Planning involves many actors, but always some governing authority with legitimacy. The approach to planning in this course involves the state as a governing authority, with the legitimate power to make changes that affect many other actors, in its unique role as the 'rule-maker' that determines the institutional framework for the operation of all other actors, while recognizing the growth of other governing actors at the local and global levels. Power also determines whether countries are able to plan, and if so, the kind of planning that they are able to do in a contested global arena. The language of legitimacy shapes how and by whom and in what form power can be exercised.

Another major theme of the course is that planning in developing countries is much more impacted by the ideas and actions of external actors, compared to planning in rich industrialized countries. This in turn makes the process of planning – the actors and institutional processes as well political sustainability – much more complex and unpredictable. The course also focuses on the history of planning and development in non-Western countries in order to better appreciate the achievements and limitations of planning efforts in the past. The impact of colonialism and imperialism on the political culture, institutional legacies, and economic prospects of developing countries needs to be understood. In focusing on this issue, the course would like to call attention to the importance of historical analysis in planning in developing countries.

Prerequisites:
There are no prerequisites for this course.

Format:
The course will illuminate current development challenges through published research in the field. The literature is rich, and across many disciplines in the social sciences. Case studies and real world examples, through interaction with planning practitioners, are drawn from around the world. The readings marked 'required' must be read carefully prior to class. Students are encouraged to prepare short outlines based on reading notes before coming to class. The readings marked 'recommended' are suggested to supplement, deepen and expand the issues raised by the required readings. While students don't have to read them all before class, they are strongly encouraged to read what they can in order to fully realize the potential of this course.

Requirements:

| Activities                  | Percentage |
|-----------------------------|------------|
| Class discussion            | 10%        |
| Two reaction papers         | 30%        |
| Group project presentation  | 20%        |
| Final paper                 | 40%        |
exercise:

This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. Exercise:
TITLE: Concepts: Pro versus Anti-Development

11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning
Massachusetts Institute of Technology

Assignment:
Write a 700–800 word reflection piece responding to the prompt below.

Prompt:
What do we make of anti-development arguments? Are they utopian or nihilist or neither? Can one be anti-development and still plan? What are the justifications for planning? Or is the division between pro and anti too Manichean? Why certain types of anti-planning arguments emerged at certain times? What was the historical context in which these criticisms emerged? What explains the rise, fall and now the latest rise of anti-development arguments?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: Hi, I’m trying to figure out this reflection on pro- vs anti-development arguments, and I’m honestly not sure how to organize my response. Could you help me sort out what “anti-development” really means in this prompt and how it might still connect to planning?
exchanges.tutor: Good starting point. Before I weigh in — what have you encountered in your readings so far that gives you a sense of what critics of 'development' are actually objecting to? What is it they seem to be against?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking for help understanding the core concept before diving into the prompt. Rather than explaining anti-development to them, I should use a Socratic question to activate their prior knowledge and get them thinking independently. I'll ask them what they already understand about it to gauge where they are and let them construct their own understanding.

exchanges.student: I think they’re not always against improving people’s lives, but more against the idea of “development” as a top-down model imposed by states or outside actors. I’m a little unsure, but it seems like the criticism is about who defines progress, who gets power, and the harm caused by projects done in the name of development.
exchanges.tutor: That's a good instinct — you've identified something important about the *locus* of power and who defines progress. Now, given that framing, think about the prompt's question: does that kind of critique make anti-development thinkers *utopian*, *nihilist*, or something else entirely? What do those two labels actually imply about someone's relationship to change and action?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has shown good initial intuition about the core critique — power, definition of progress, and external imposition. This is solid ground. I should affirm the direction without praising excessively, then push them to go deeper with a Socratic question that connects this understanding to the prompt's specific questions about utopianism, nihilism, and planning.

exchanges.student: I’m stuck between them a bit. “Utopian” makes me think they want a totally different social order, while “nihilist” sounds like they reject all collective action or think improvement is impossible. My guess is a lot of anti-development arguments are neither, because they may reject dominant development models without rejecting change itself.
exchanges.tutor: You're thinking clearly here. So if anti-development critics aren't rejecting change itself, just the dominant *form* it takes — what does that imply for planning? Does rejecting a particular model of development necessarily mean rejecting planning altogether?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is reasoning well and arriving at a nuanced position. I should affirm the direction and push them to connect this insight to the next logical question in the prompt: if anti-development thinkers aren't nihilists (i.e., they don't reject change itself), what does that mean for planning? Can one still plan while rejecting dominant development models? This moves the conversation forward toward the core tension in the assignment.

exchanges.student: I don’t think so, but this is where I get fuzzy. Maybe they reject technocratic or state-led planning, but could still support more local, participatory, or alternative forms of planning? So anti-development might be anti-certain kinds of planning, not anti-planning in every sense.
exchanges.tutor: That's a strong insight and worth holding onto for your essay. Now the prompt also asks *why* certain anti-development arguments emerged at certain times. What do you know about the historical moments when these critiques became prominent? What was happening in the world that might have made people question dominant development models?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has made a strong conceptual leap connecting anti-development to alternative planning forms. This is a key insight for the essay. I should affirm it briefly and then redirect toward another part of the prompt that they haven't touched yet — the historical context question. This will help them build a more complete essay argument and avoid dwelling too long on this one concept.

exchanges.student: I think postcolonial experience matters a lot, since many countries saw “development” tied to outside control even after formal independence. Also maybe the failures of big modernization projects, debt crises, structural adjustment, and inequality made people doubt the promise of development. I’m not fully sure how to connect the timing, though.
exchanges.tutor: You've got the right ingredients. Now try to be more precise: when development projects *failed* visibly — debt crises, structural adjustment, growing inequality — what effect might that have had on intellectuals, planners, and affected communities? Why would *failure* generate a theoretical critique rather than just a call for better implementation?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has identified good historical markers but is struggling to connect timing to the emergence of critique. I should push them to think more precisely about the relationship between historical failures and the rise of anti-development thought, without giving them the answer. A targeted question about cause and effect will help them make that connection themselves.

exchanges.student: Maybe because repeated failure makes people think the problem is structural, not just bad execution. Like, if the same kinds of projects keep producing dependency, displacement, or inequality, then critics start questioning the whole idea of development, not just the management of it. That part actually clicks more now.
exchanges.tutor: Good — that's a meaningful distinction between execution failures and structural critique. Now the prompt also asks whether the pro vs. anti divide is too 'Manichean.' Given everything you've worked out, do you think that binary actually holds up, or does it obscure something more complicated?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has made a strong logical leap connecting systemic failure to structural critique. This is excellent reasoning. We've now covered several key parts of the prompt. I should briefly affirm and then push toward the remaining unaddressed parts of the prompt — the Manichean division question and the 'rise, fall, and latest rise' of anti-development arguments — to help them think about the full arc of their essay.

exchanges.student: Yeah, I think the binary is probably too simple. Someone can criticize mainstream development and still care about redistribution, welfare, dignity, or planning, so “anti-development” can hide a lot of differences. I’m still not sure how to phrase that without sounding vague.
exchanges.tutor: Don't worry about phrasing yet — clarity in writing usually follows clarity in thinking. There's one part of the prompt you haven't touched: the *rise, fall, and latest rise* of anti-development arguments. What do you think explains that arc? Why might these critiques fade at certain moments and resurge at others?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has good conceptual clarity but is worried about vagueness in expression. My role is not to suggest language or polish their writing — that's explicitly prohibited. Instead, I should redirect them toward the last major unaddressed part of the prompt: the 'rise, fall, and latest rise' of anti-development arguments. This will help them build a fuller essay. I should also note that the concern about vagueness will resolve itself once they have a concrete argument to anchor it.

exchanges.student: I think they rise when mainstream development looks especially damaging or discredited, and maybe fade when growth optimism comes back or when institutions repackage criticism into softer language like participation or sustainability. Then they rise again when newer crises show the underlying power problems never really went away. Could you help me turn that into a cleaner argument?
exchanges.tutor: That's actually a solid argument — I won't help shape the language for you, but you don't need me to. You've worked out the core ideas across this conversation. At this point, I'd encourage you to try writing a first draft. What would your opening claim be?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to help them write or polish their argument, which I must refuse — that's explicitly prohibited. However, their reasoning is genuinely strong and covers most of the prompt. We've now had 7 exchanges and covered most of the key conceptual ground. I should refuse the writing request clearly but constructively, and instead push them to attempt a written draft on their own, integrating everything we've discussed.

exchanges.student: I think my opening claim would be that anti-development arguments are not simply utopian or nihilist, but historically grounded critiques of how development has been defined and practiced. I want to say they often reject dominant, top-down models while still leaving room for planning aimed at justice or local autonomy.
exchanges.tutor: That's a strong opening claim — it's specific and arguable. As you draft, make sure you also weave in the historical arc and the Manichean point you worked out earlier; those are explicitly part of the prompt and would strengthen the argument considerably. Go ahead and try a full draft — you have the material.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has articulated a strong opening claim that integrates most of what we've discussed. This is a good moment to affirm the progress and encourage them to move to drafting. I should not grade or polish, but can note the strengths and point to what still needs to be woven in — specifically the historical arc (rise, fall, rise) and the Manichean critique — to make sure they build a complete essay.

exchanges.student: Okay, I’ll try that. Before I draft, can I quickly check if this structure makes sense: define anti-development as critique of dominant models, argue it’s neither utopian nor nihilist, explain how it can still allow planning, then use the historical arc to show why the binary keeps reappearing?
exchanges.tutor: That structure is logical and covers most of the prompt. One thing to check: does your historical arc section also explain the *specific* contexts that generated the critiques — colonialism, structural adjustment, etc. — or does it only describe the pattern of rise and fall? The prompt asks for both. Now start drafting.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is proposing a structure that is logical and covers most of the prompt. I can briefly affirm it, but I should also note one gap — the historical *context* in which critiques emerged (colonialism, structural adjustment, etc.) seems to be missing as a distinct element, though it may be embedded in the arc. I should point this out without being too prescriptive, and then push them to start writing.
